summary: Terrie E. Moffitt outlines her developmental taxonomy of antisocial behavior, which posits that the age-crime curve conceals two distinct groups: life-course persistent (LCP) and adolescence-limited (AL) offenders. LCP behavior is rare, originates in early childhood from neuropsychological and environmental risks, and persists into adulthood. AL behavior is common, emerges during puberty due to a 'maturity gap,' and naturally desists as individuals assume adult roles.

principles.statement: The age-crime curve conceals two distinct groups of offenders with entirely different causes.
principles.rationale: Antisocial behavior either originates early in life due to neuropsychological and environmental risks (Life-Course Persistent) or emerges during puberty as a normative response to the maturity gap (Adolescence-Limited).
principles.source_quote: The developmental taxonomy proposed that the age-crime curve conceals two groups, and proffered two distinct theories.

principles.statement: Adolescence-limited antisocial behavior is common and normative, while abstaining is rare.
principles.rationale: During the 'maturity gap' between biological maturation and access to adult privileges, it is virtually normative for healthy youngsters to emulate delinquent lifestyles to demonstrate autonomy and win peer affiliation.
principles.source_quote: In fact, adolescent abstainers from offending are rare.

principles.statement: Life-course persistent antisocial behavior diminishes the possibility of reform over time.
principles.rationale: As antisocial behavior infiltrates multiple adult life domains (employment, relationships, legal status), the accumulating consequences make it increasingly difficult for the individual to change course.
principles.source_quote: This cumulative infiltration gradually diminishes the possibility of reform, accounting for the persistence of LCP behavior.

frameworks.name: Developmental Taxonomy of Antisocial Behavior
frameworks.when_to_apply: When analyzing the causes, prevention strategies, and life-course trajectories of antisocial behavior and crime.
steps: Determine the onset of the antisocial behavior: early childhood (LCP) or alongside puberty (AL).
For early-onset (LCP), assess for inherited or acquired neuropsychological problems combined with a high-risk social environment.
For adolescent-onset (AL), recognize the behavior as a response to the 'maturity gap' by otherwise healthy youngsters.
Predict desistance for AL individuals as they age into real adult roles, and persistence for LCP individuals as their behavior infiltrates adult domains.
frameworks.source_quote: The developmental taxonomy proposed that the age-crime curve conceals two groups, and proffered two distinct theories.

mental_models.name: The Maturity Gap
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding adolescent delinquency. It describes the psychological discomfort youths experience during the relatively role-less years between their biological maturation (puberty) and their access to mature privileges and responsibilities. Delinquency is used to bridge this gap and demonstrate autonomy.
mental_models.example: Young people becoming dissatisfied with their childlike dependent status and emulating a delinquent lifestyle to hasten social maturation.

mental_models.name: Cumulative Infiltration
mental_models.description: A model explaining the persistence of severe antisocial behavior. Over decades, transactions between a high-risk individual and their environment construct a disordered personality that infiltrates multiple adult domains (illegal activities, substance misuse, employment problems), which locks them into that lifestyle.
mental_models.example: An individual's early aggressive behavior leads to poor peer relationships, missed prosocial skills, and eventually problems with employment and intimate partner victimization, making reform nearly impossible.

heuristics: Adolescent abstainers from offending are rare; some delinquency is virtually normative.

signature_quotes.text: This article reviews recent evidence regarding the hypothesis that the age-crime curve conceals two groups with different causes.
signature_quotes.context: Introducing the core premise of her developmental taxonomy of antisocial behavior.

signature_quotes.text: In fact, adolescent abstainers from offending are rare.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining that adolescence-limited antisocial behavior is a common, normative part of development.

signature_quotes.text: This cumulative infiltration gradually diminishes the possibility of reform, accounting for the persistence of LCP behavior.
signature_quotes.context: Describing why life-course persistent offenders fail to desist from crime in adulthood.

signature_quotes.text: ...otherwise ordinary healthy youngsters experience psychological discomfort during the relatively role-less years between their biological maturation and their access to mature privileges and responsibilities, a period termed the 'maturity gap.'
signature_quotes.context: Defining the root cause of adolescence-limited antisocial behavior.

anti_patterns.description: Treating all adolescent antisocial behavior as pathological or indicative of a lifelong criminal trajectory.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores that the age-crime curve conceals two groups. Most adolescent offenders are 'adolescence-limited'—their behavior is a normative response to the maturity gap, their pre-delinquent development was healthy, and they will naturally desist when they age into adult roles.
